title: Rainbow fractional matchings
speaker: Ron Holzman
abstract: Given a family of m matchings in a graph G (where each matching can be thought of as a color class), a rainbow matching is a choice of edges of distinct colors that forms a matching. How many matchings of size n are needed to guarantee the existence of a rainbow matching of size n? If G is bipartite, a theorem of Drisko generalized by Aharoni and Berger says that m = 2n - 1 suffices (and is best possible). In a general graph G, this is not the case, but m = 2n is conjectured to be enough.

proof_stages.label: Rainbow matchings and the bipartite benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces rainbow matchings, states Drisko's theorem that 2n-1 matchings of size n in a bipartite graph force a rainbow matching of size n, and reviews its sharpness and related applications.

proof_stages.label: Non-bipartite obstruction and integral conjecture
proof_stages.summary: A non-bipartite construction shows that the bipartite 2n-1 statement fails in general graphs, motivating Aharoni's conjecture that 2n matchings suffice and contrasting it with the known 3n-2 bound.

proof_stages.label: Fractional and rainbow fractional matchings in graphs
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines fractional matching number and rainbow fractional matching, then states the graph theorem that 2n edge-sets with fractional matching number at least n admit a rainbow fractional matching of size n, with 2n-1 sufficing in the bipartite case.

proof_stages.label: Hypergraph formulation
proof_stages.summary: The graph theorem is generalized to r-uniform hypergraphs: ceil(rn) edge-sets suffice in general, while ceil(rn)-r+1 suffice in the r-partite case, recovering Drisko when r=2 and the graph is bipartite.

proof_stages.label: Need for a topological proof method
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that graph-specific structural and augmenting-path arguments do not extend to hypergraphs because fractional matchings there have arbitrary structure, so the proof uses topology.

proof_stages.label: Kalai–Meshulam colorful selection theorem
proof_stages.summary: A partition-matroid special case of Kalai–Meshulam's theorem is stated: for a d-Leray simplicial complex, if each part is outside the complex, then one can choose a transversal also outside the complex.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to Leray bounds for fractional-matching complexes
proof_stages.summary: The proof constructs a simplicial complex whose faces are edge-sets with fractional matching number below n, applies Kalai–Meshulam to select rainbow edges, and reduces the main theorem to proving the required Leray bound for this complex.

proof_stages.label: Topological barrier for the integral problem
proof_stages.summary: Replacing fractional matching number by ordinary matching number in the complex cannot yield the conjectural 2n result by the same topological route, because examples rule out the stronger Leray property needed and only align with the known 3n-2 bound.

examples.name: Alternating perfect matchings on a 2n-cycle
examples.note: Taking n-1 copies of each of the two perfect matchings of an even cycle demonstrates why Drisko's 2n-1 bound cannot be lowered in the bipartite setting.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the 2n-cycle with red and blue alternating perfect matchings and argues by the structure of size-n matchings why no rainbow matching exists with only 2n−2 copies

passes.reason: repeated copies of the two alternating perfect matchings on a highly symmetric even cycle give the tight obstruction to 2n-2 sufficing
The repeated two alternating perfect matchings on an even cycle form a tight, highly collapsed lower-bound construction against using only 2n-2 matchings.
The repeated two alternating perfect matchings on a highly regular even cycle form the sharp obstruction to lowering Drisko’s 2n−1 bound.
The repeated two alternating perfect matchings on a highly symmetric 2n-cycle form the sharp 2n-2 obstruction to Drisko's bound.
the highly regular even cycle with its two alternating perfect matchings gives the tight obstruction showing 2n−2 matchings do not suffice
n-1 repeated copies of the two highly regular alternating perfect matchings on C_{2n} give the sharp one-below-Drisko obstruction.
The repeated two alternating perfect matchings on a highly symmetric cycle witness sharp failure with 2n−2 colors.
Repeated copies of the two alternating perfect matchings of C_{2n} form a highly regular tight lower-bound obstruction to 2n−2 sufficing.
used to show Drisko’s 2n−1 bound cannot be lowered; the alternating 1-factors of C_{2n} form a tight extremal construction.
the alternating perfect matchings on C_{2n} give a highly regular tight lower-bound obstruction with 2n-2 colors and no size-n rainbow matching.
A degenerate multiset of repeated copies of the two alternating perfect matchings of a highly symmetric even cycle gives the tight obstruction to lowering Drisko's 2n-1 bound.
The two alternating perfect matchings of C_{2n}, repeated n−1 times each, form the tight obstruction to lowering Drisko’s 2n−1 bound.

examples.name: Non-bipartite cycle with an added yellow perfect matching
examples.note: Adding a third type of perfect matching to the cycle construction gives a concrete obstruction to the 2n-1 bound in non-bipartite graphs and visibly motivates the conjectural 2n threshold.

examples.name: Triangle with half weight on each edge
examples.note: The triangle shows that a fractional matching can have size 3/2 while the ordinary matching number is only 1, clarifying why the fractional relaxation differs in non-bipartite graphs.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker assigns weight 1/2 to each triangle edge and computes the fractional matching size as 3/2 while comparing it to the ordinary matching number 1

passes.reason: the symmetric triangle with half weight on every edge attains the graph fractional-matching gap and refutes integral equality in general graphs
The uniformly half-weighted triangle is the symmetric minimal odd-cycle example showing fractional matching number can exceed matching number and attaining the graph integrality-gap ratio.
The uniformly half-weighted triangle is a maximally symmetric example showing ν* can exceed ν and attaining the graph fractional/integral gap 3/2.
The equal half-weighted triangle is symmetric and attains the maximum 3/2 graph integrality gap, refuting equality of integral and fractional matching numbers in general graphs.

The highly symmetric triangle with weights 1/2 on all edges refutes integrality of matching number and attains the maximal graph gap.
The symmetric triangle with half weight on each edge is the minimal odd-cycle example attaining the maximal graph fractional/integral matching gap.

examples.name: Convex-set nerve and colorful Helly theorem
examples.note: The nerve complex of convex sets in Euclidean space and Lovász's colorful Helly theorem illustrate the d-Leray condition and the motivation behind Kalai–Meshulam's theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes the convex-set nerve interpretation and explains how the Kalai–Meshulam theorem applied to it yields the colorful Helly conclusion

passes.reason: standard convex-set nerve used to motivate the d-Leray condition and recover colorful Helly
